title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: , estimate logistic regression 1000000 observations 50 covariates. vary number cores used nthreads argument parglm.control. method argument sets method used. LINPACK uses pivoted QR (dqrdc2) glm.fit final decomposition; LAPACK uses DGEQP3 LAPACK throughout; FAST solves normal equations Fisher information, similar speedglm package. plot shows median run times versus number cores. Coloured horizontal lines show single-threaded reference times glm, speedglm, glm2, fastglm, bigglm, bam. used glm.fit parglm.fit. make relative difference bigger call e.g., model.matrix model.frame take time. show point, first compute much time takes make plot. black solid line computation time model.matrix model.frame. Plot runtime versus number cores. open circles FAST method filled circles LINPACK method. FAST method, speedglm, fastglm compute Fisher information solve normal equation. advantage terms computation cost may lead unstable solutions. can alter number observations parallel chunk block_size argument parglm.control. single threaded performance parglm may slower coefficients. cause seems difference LAPACK LINPACK implementation. presumably due either QR decomposition method /qr.qty method. Windows, parglm seem slower built Rtools reason seems qr.qty method LAPACK, dormqr, slower LINPACK method, dqrsl. illustration computation times machine.
code: ##### # simulate n # number of observations #> [1] 1000000 p # number of covariates #> [1] 50 set.seed(68024947) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, 1/p, 1/sqrt(p)), n, ncol = p) df <- data.frame(y = 1/(1 + exp(-(rowSums(X) - 1))) > runif(n), X) ##### # compute and measure time. Setup call to make library(microbenchmark) library(speedglm) library(fastglm) library(glm2) library(biglm) library(mgcv) library(parglm) X_mat <- model.matrix(y ~ ., df) biglm_form <- reformulate(names(df)[-1], response = "y") bam_form   <- biglm_form cl <- list(   quote(microbenchmark),   glm      = quote(glm     (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   speedglm = quote(speedglm(y ~ ., family = binomial(), data = df)),   glm2     = quote(glm2    (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   fastglm  = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial())),   bigglm   = quote(bigglm  (biglm_form, df, family = binomial())),   bam      = quote(bam     (bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df)),   times = 11L) tfunc <- function(method = "LINPACK", nthreads)   parglm(y ~ ., binomial(), df, control = parglm.control(method = method,                                                          nthreads = nthreads)) cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(nthreads = .(i))))) names(cl)[9:(9L + n_threads - 1L)] <- paste0("parglm-LINPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "FAST")))) names(cl)[(9L + n_threads):(9L + 2L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-FAST-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "LAPACK")))) names(cl)[(9L + 2L * n_threads):(9L + 3L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-LAPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- as.call(cl) cl # the call we make #> microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~  #>     ., family = binomial(), data = df), glm2 = glm2(y ~ ., binomial(),  #>     df), fastglm = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial()),  #>     bigglm = bigglm(biglm_form, df, family = binomial()), bam = bam(bam_form,  #>         family = binomial(), data = df), times = 11L, `parglm-LINPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L), `parglm-LINPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L), `parglm-LINPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L), `parglm-LINPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L), `parglm-LINPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L), `parglm-FAST-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-LAPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"))  out <- eval(cl) #> Warning in microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~ : #> less accurate nanosecond times to avoid potential integer overflows s <- summary(out) # result from `microbenchmark` print(s[, c("expr", "min", "mean", "median", "max")], digits  = 3,       row.names = FALSE) #>               expr   min  mean median   max #>                glm  5159  5367   5300  5792 #>           speedglm  3913  4089   4067  4251 #>               glm2  5207  5467   5428  5737 #>            fastglm  1914  2073   2031  2520 #>             bigglm  6489  6610   6612  6762 #>                bam 11670 11980  11948 12231 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1  6465  6669   6662  6880 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2  3696  3789   3782  3881 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3  2805  2916   2853  3221 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4  2416  2479   2467  2534 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5  2074  2209   2238  2313 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6  1913  1963   1936  2173 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7  1852  1987   1878  2305 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8  1790  1868   1870  2015 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9  1752  1820   1818  1905 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10  1739  1841   1823  2027 #>      parglm-FAST-1  3602  3799   3737  4223 #>      parglm-FAST-2  2012  2090   2074  2230 #>      parglm-FAST-3  1525  1649   1592  1918 #>      parglm-FAST-4  1218  1300   1272  1446 #>      parglm-FAST-5  1104  1177   1149  1350 #>      parglm-FAST-6  1005  1093   1077  1183 #>      parglm-FAST-7   969  1005    981  1161 #>      parglm-FAST-8   874   966    956  1076 #>      parglm-FAST-9   891   964    976  1040 #>     parglm-FAST-10   847   982    958  1223 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1  6512  6659   6643  6839 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2  3710  3806   3794  3918 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3  2835  2929   2874  3258 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4  2465  2533   2541  2641 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5  2065  2274   2173  3156 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6  1931  1978   1973  2085 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7  1825  1950   1958  2153 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8  1803  1878   1832  2071 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9  1733  1880   1842  2254 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10  1751  1869   1830  2232 modmat_time <- microbenchmark(   modmat_time = {     mf <- model.frame(y ~ ., df); model.matrix(terms(mf), mf)   }, times = 10) modmat_time # time taken by `model.matrix` and `model.frame` #> Unit: milliseconds #>         expr       min       lq        mean    median         uq       max neval #>  modmat_time 76.536463 95.35575 108.1825631 97.408825 120.252836 158.53347    10 par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, .5, 9)) o <- aggregate(time ~ expr, out, median)[, 2] / 10^9 ylim <- range(o, 0); ylim[2] <- ylim[2] + .04 * diff(ylim)  o_linpack <- o[7L:(n_threads + 6L)] o_fast    <- o[(n_threads + 7L):(2L * n_threads + 6L)] o_lapack  <- o[(2L * n_threads + 7L):(3L * n_threads + 6L)]  ref_cols <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7") ref_lty  <- c("dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", "longdash", "twodash", "1373")  plot(1:n_threads, o_linpack, xlab = "Number of cores", yaxs = "i",      ylim = ylim, ylab = "Run time (s)", pch = 16, cex = 1.4) points(1:n_threads, o_fast,   pch = 1, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) points(1:n_threads, o_lapack, pch = 4, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) for (i in 1:6)   abline(h = o[i], lty = ref_lty[i], col = ref_cols[i], lwd = 2) abline(h = median(modmat_time$time) / 10^9, lty = "solid", col = "black",        lwd = 2) usr <- par("usr") par(xpd = TRUE) legend(x = usr[2], y = usr[4], xjust = 0, yjust = 1,        legend = c("glm", "speedglm", "glm2", "fastglm", "bigglm", "bam",                   "model.matrix", "parglm LINPACK", "parglm FAST",                   "parglm LAPACK"),        lty = c(ref_lty, "solid", NA, NA, NA),        lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA),        col = c(ref_cols, "black", "black", "black", "black"),        pch = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 16, 1, 4),        bty = "n") qr1 <- qr(X) qr2 <- qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   `qr LINPACK`     = qr(X),   `qr LAPACK`      = qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE),   `qr.qty LINPACK` = qr.qty(qr1, df$y),   `qr.qty LAPACK`  = qr.qty(qr2, df$y),   times = 11) #> Unit: milliseconds #>            expr         min           lq          mean      median          uq #>      qr LINPACK 1230.333248 1237.3688275 1247.89698455 1240.458444 1255.463603 #>       qr LAPACK 1412.945649 1422.6686145 1436.03165318 1425.796812 1443.483618 #>  qr.qty LINPACK   77.531164   82.7846375   88.05243864   84.320477   90.102707 #>   qr.qty LAPACK  554.873705  558.2122325  565.13979564  561.589300  567.038364 #>          max neval #>  1282.100873    11 #>  1495.966139    11 #>   114.143057    11 #>   599.499827    11

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: smaller datasets per-call overhead parglm’s thread pool can exceed gains parallelism. repeat benchmark n = 100,000 n = 10,000, dropping bam since aimed much larger problems. Note default block_size parglm.control 10000, n = 10,000 work split across threads parglm effectively runs single-threaded regardless nthreads.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 10,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: per-row cost method scales roughly \(p^2\) (computing \(X'WX\) IRLS iteration), parglm’s thread-pool overhead roughly fixed per call. two effects compete: small \(p\) overhead dominates parallel benefit, larger \(p\) parallel work amortizes overhead parglm pulls ahead. contrast n = 100,000, p = 5 (overhead wins) n = 1,000,000, p = 20 (parallel work wins).

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000 p = 5.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 1,000,000 p = 20.

title: Parallel GLM
text: parglm package provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS. can install release version CRAN install development version GitHub calling: See vignette example run times details.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Maintainer: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Authors: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Benjamin Christoffersen boennecd@gmail.com (ORCID) contributors: Anthony Williams [copyright holder] Boost developers [copyright holder]

what: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Auxiliary function parglm fitting.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: parglm.control(   epsilon = 1e-08,   maxit = 25,   trace = FALSE,   nthreads = parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L),   block_size = NULL,   method = "LINPACK" )

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: epsilon positive convergence tolerance. maxit integer giving maximal number IWLS iterations. trace logical indicating output produced estimation. nthreads number cores use. Defaults parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L), leaves one core free. may get best performance using available physical cores data set sufficiently large. block_size number observations include parallel block. method string specifying method use. Either "LINPACK", "LAPACK", "FAST".

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: list components named arguments.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: LINPACK method uses QR method glm.fit final QR decomposition. dqrdc2 method described qr. QR decompositions last made DGEQP3 LAPACK. See Wood, Goude, Shaw (2015) details QR method. FAST method computes Fisher information solves normal equation. faster less numerically stable.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Wood, S.N., Goude, Y. & Shaw S. (2015) Generalized additive models large datasets. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series C 64(1): 139-155.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: # use one core f1 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  # use two cores (mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: Function like glm can make computation parallel. function supports families listed family. See "vignette("parglm", "parglm")" run time examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: parglm(   formula,   family = gaussian,   data,   weights,   subset,   na.action,   start = NULL,   offset,   control = list(...),   contrasts = NULL,   model = TRUE,   x = FALSE,   y = TRUE,   ... )  parglm.fit(   x,   y,   weights = rep(1, NROW(x)),   start = NULL,   etastart = NULL,   mustart = NULL,   offset = rep(0, NROW(x)),   family = gaussian(),   control = list(),   intercept = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: formula object class formula. family family object. data optional data frame, list environment containing variables model. weights optional vector 'prior weights' used fitting process. NULL numeric vector. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs. start starting values parameters linear predictor. offset can used specify priori known component included linear predictor fitting. control list parameters controlling fitting process. parglm.fit passed parglm.control. contrasts optional list. See contrasts.arg model.matrix.default. model logical value indicating whether model frame included component returned value. x, y parglm: logical values indicating whether response vector model matrix used fitting process returned components returned value. parglm.fit: x design matrix dimension n * p, y vector observations length n. ... parglm: arguments used form default control argument supplied directly. parglm.fit: unused. etastart starting values linear predictor. supported. mustart starting values vector means. supported. intercept logical. intercept included null model?

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: glm object returned glm differs mainly qr element. qr element object returned parglm(.fit) \(R\) matrix QR decomposition.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: current implementation uses min(.integer(n / p), nthreads) threads n number observations, p number covariates, nthreads nthreads element list returned parglm.control. Thus, likely little () reduction computation time p almost equal n. current implementation handle p > n.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: # mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads (>= 16 rows per thread) f1 <- glm   (mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

title: parglm (development version)
text: Add biglm, fastglm, glm2, mgcv timing comparison vignette. add timings fewer observations fewer coefficients. Bump roxygen2 8.0.0 Remove busy-wait polling qr_parallel::get_stacks_res Return eta mu parallelglm skip R-side recomputation Sum upper triangle per-chunk Fisher information FAST path Drop const R_F members enable move semantics Hoist NA-zeroing beta per-chunk workers Use thread_local index thread_pool::get_id Skip intermediate matrix reverse-pivoting R Cache parglm_supported keys avoid rebuilding family objects per fit Change default parglm.control(nthreads) parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L) make better use available cores

title: parglm 0.1.8
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-21 Remove specification C++11 Amend std::result_of std::invoke_result header file Fix infinite recursion QR_base::qyt removing std::move Require C++17 Linux, macOS, Windows New maintainer - Tom Palmer

title: parglm 0.1.7
text: CRAN release: 2021-10-14 Avoid virtual function calls remove macros. Fix issue due new STRICT_R_HEADERS variable Rcpp.

title: parglm 0.1.6
text: CRAN release: 2020-08-10 Avoid memory allocations. Fix test issue ATLAS.

title: parglm 0.1.4
text: CRAN release: 2020-01-07 stops variables observations. Previously, caused crash. Handle Fortran string length argument.

title: parglm 0.1.3
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-18 Fix bug found Valgrind.

title: parglm 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-14 Minor changes implementation. Fix bugs patched R oldrel R.

title: parglm 0.1.1
text: CRAN release: 2019-01-19 FAST method added computes Fisher information solves normal equation speedglm. One change decreased computation time. Minor bug fixes.
